subsections.title: JDBC Core & Connection Pooling
subsections.keyRule: JDBC = Java Database Connectivity — the standard API for all relational DBs. Always use PreparedStatement (not Statement) to prevent SQL injection. Always close connections with try- with-resources. Never create new connections per request — use a connection pool (HikariCP is the fastest).

terms.what: Statement executes a raw SQL string. PreparedStatement precompiles SQL with ? placeholders for parameters — safer and faster.
terms.why: Building SQL with String concatenation (Statement) allows SQL injection attacks. PreparedStatement treats parameters as data, never as SQL.
terms.how: PreparedStatement ps = conn.prepareStatement('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?'); ps.setLong(1, userId); ResultSet rs = ps.executeQuery();
terms.where: Every parameterized SQL query. Batch operations. Any query with user-provided input.
terms.when: ALWAYS use PreparedStatement. NEVER use Statement for queries with parameters. Only use Statement for static DDL or queries with no parameters.
terms.issue: Statement with string concat: 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ''' + name + '''' — if name = '' OR '1'='1', returns all users. Classic SQL injection.
terms.fix: PreparedStatement parameterizes all inputs. The DB treats them as values, not SQL. No injection possible.

star.s: Login form vulnerable to SQL injection — '' OR '1'='1' bypasses password.

terms.what: Transaction groups multiple SQL operations so they ALL succeed or ALL fail together. ACID: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability.
terms.why: Without transactions, partial updates corrupt data. Transfer money: debit one account, credit another — if credit fails, debit must be rolled back.
terms.how: conn.setAutoCommit(false); try { stmt1.execute(); stmt2.execute(); conn.commit(); } catch(Exception e) { conn.rollback(); }
terms.where: Any multi-step data operation: transfers, order placement, inventory updates, user registration.
terms.when: Use transactions for: any operation that updates multiple tables or rows that must stay consistent. In Spring, use @Transactional — much simpler.
terms.issue: Pool too small = requests queue waiting for connection = slow. Pool too large = DB overwhelmed = slow. Pool size mismatch with DB side.
terms.fix: Rule of thumb: pool size = (core_count * 2) + disk spindle count. Monitor pool wait time. Increase pool only if wait time is consistently high.

star.s: App slow under load — requests taking 2 seconds.
star.a: HikariCP wait time 1800ms — pool size 5 too small for 100 concurrent users. Increased to 20.

terms.what: ResultSet holds the result of a SQL SELECT query. You iterate it row by row with rs.next(), and read each column with rs.getString(), rs.getInt(), etc.
terms.why: SQL returns tabular data. ResultSet provides cursor-based iteration over that data.
terms.how: while (rs.next()) { Long id = rs.getLong('id'); String name = rs.getString('name'); } Always close ResultSet, Statement, and Connection.
terms.where: Every SELECT query in JDBC code.
terms.when: Always close ResultSet in try-with-resources. For large datasets, use rs.setFetchSize() to fetch in batches instead of loading all rows into memory.
terms.issue: Loading 1 million rows into ResultSet without fetchSize = all 1M rows in memory = OOM.
terms.fix: ps.setFetchSize(500) — fetch 500 rows at a time. Or better, use Spring Data with pagination (Pageable) to limit result size.

star.s: Export report OOM on large table.
star.a: Added rs.setFetchSize(1000). Process and write each batch without holding all in memory.
star.r: Export any size table without OOM.

subsections.title: JDBC Extended
subsections.keyRule: DriverManager = simple connection factory for testing. DataSource = production connection pool. CallableStatement = stored procedures. executeBatch = bulk operations. ACID = Atomicity/Consistency/Isolation/Durability. Isolation levels control dirty/non-repeatable/phantom read phenomena.

terms.what: CallableStatement executes stored procedures in the database. Extends PreparedStatement. Supports IN, OUT, and INOUT parameters.
terms.why: When business logic lives in stored procedures, CallableStatement is how Java calls them.
terms.how: CallableStatement cs = con.prepareCall('{call get_customer(?, ?)}'); cs.setInt(1, customerId); cs.registerOutParameter(2, Types.VARCHAR); cs.execute(); String name = cs.getString(2);
terms.where: Legacy enterprise systems with stored procedures, Oracle PL/SQL calls, SQL Server stored proc calls.
terms.when: Use when calling database stored procedures. For new code, prefer business logic in Java and simple CRUD SQL.
terms.issue: CallableStatement tightly couples Java code to stored procedure signatures — hard to change either side independently.
terms.fix: Document stored procedure contracts. Version them. Consider moving logic to Java if possible.

star.s: Legacy Oracle system — customer data only accessible via stored proc GET_CUSTOMER(id, out name, out email).

terms.what: executeQuery() for SELECT — returns ResultSet. executeUpdate() for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE — returns int (rows affected). execute() for any SQL — returns boolean (true=ResultSet, false=update count).
terms.why: Choosing the right method catches misuse errors. executeQuery() on an UPDATE would throw exception in some drivers.
terms.how: ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery('SELECT...'); int rows = stmt.executeUpdate('INSERT...'); boolean isResultSet = stmt.execute('...');
terms.where: All JDBC Statement and PreparedStatement usage.
terms.when: Always use the specific method: executeQuery for SELECTs, executeUpdate for DML. Use execute() only when query type is not known in advance.

terms.what: ACID: Atomicity (all or nothing), Consistency (DB rules always satisfied), Isolation (transactions don't see each other's incomplete work), Durability (committed data survives crashes).
terms.why: ACID guarantees make database transactions reliable. Without it, concurrent transactions and crashes could leave data in inconsistent states.
terms.how: Atomicity: transaction commits entirely or rolls back entirely. Consistency: foreign keys, constraints always valid. Isolation: set by isolation level. Durability: write-ahead log ensures committed data survives crash.
terms.where: Relational databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle). NoSQL often trades ACID for availability (BASE).
terms.when: Understand ACID when designing transactions, choosing isolation level, or evaluating database technology.
terms.issue: Long transactions can hold locks too long, reducing concurrency. High isolation levels can cause deadlocks.
terms.fix: Use savepoints sparingly. Release them when no longer needed. Prefer well- designed smaller transactions over complex savepoint logic.

star.s: Transaction that must save headers but allow row-level errors.
star.a: Savepoint before each row. On row error: rollback to savepoint, log error, continue.
star.r: Partial import — valid rows saved, invalid logged. ACID Properties

terms.what: Four isolation levels: READ_UNCOMMITTED, READ_COMMITTED, REPEATABLE_READ, SERIALIZABLE. Each trades concurrency for protection against read phenomena: dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, phantom reads.
terms.why: Choosing wrong isolation level causes incorrect data reads (too low) or performance bottlenecks (too high).
terms.how: READ_UNCOMMITTED: dirty reads possible. READ_COMMITTED (default in most DBs): no dirty reads. REPEATABLE_READ: no dirty or non-repeatable reads. SERIALIZABLE: full isolation — no phenomena.
terms.when: Use READ_COMMITTED for most OLTP apps — good balance. Use REPEATABLE_READ for financial reads that must not change mid-transaction. Avoid SERIALIZABLE — too slow.
terms.issue: Dirty read: read uncommitted data from another transaction that rolls back. Non- repeatable read: same row read twice, different values. Phantom read: range query returns different rows on second read.
terms.fix: Set isolation level based on what you can tolerate. Dirty read: use READ_COMMITTED. Non-repeatable: REPEATABLE_READ. Phantom: SERIALIZABLE or application-level locking.

star.s: Financial report reads account balance twice — different values.
star.a: Transaction isolation was READ_COMMITTED — another transaction updated in between. Changed to REPEATABLE_READ.
